coredata.dc:title: Portable smartphone platform integrated with paper strip-assisted fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive and visual quantitation of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunhua
Xu, Xiao
Wang, Fuli
Zhao, Yue
Shi, Yubo
Zhao, Xin
Liu, Jue
coredata.dc:description: It is significant to develop low-cost, sensitive, and portable sensing platform for point-of-care testing of ascorbic acid (AA) in food quality evaluation. Herein, a smart point-of-care (SPOC) sensor, consisting of fluorescent paper chip, 3D printed-accessories and smartphone, was developed for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of AA. Fluorescent paper chip was made through printing silicon doped carbon dots (SiCDs)-Fe3+ as “ink” onto filter paper, in which SiCDs emitting strong fluorescence signal quenched by Fe3+, and then recovered by AA owing to releasing –NH2/–OH and introducing defects on SiCDs. The smartphone with an application named “AA-Tester” was established to analyze AA and exhibited high sensitivity with low detection limits of 18.12 nmol/L. HPLC method was used to verify the SPOC sensor and obtained satisfactory accuracy with relative standard deviations of 0.79–2.31 %. The portable sensing platform integrated fluorescence sensor with smartphone-device will be favorable for ultrasensitive and on-the-spot detection of AA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Smartphone-based sensor
Portable sensing platform
Fluorescence detection
Chemical sensor
Food quality

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of AA in food samples
Construction and application of smart point-of-care sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University ( 521000981407 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images of the as prepared SiCDs; (b) XRD patterns of SiCDs; (c) XPS spectra of SiCDs; XPS spectrum of O 1s (d), N 1s (e), C 1s (f), Si 2p (g) and S 2p (h); (i) UV–vis absorption, FL excitation, and FL emission spectra of SiCDs (inset: the photographs of before (I) and after (II) excitation with 365 nm UV lamp).

(a) FL spectra of SiCDs, SiCDs + AA, SiCDs + Fe3+ and SiCDs + Fe3++AA; (b) UV–vis absorption (black line) of Fe3+, FL excitation (purple line), and FL emission (green line) spectra of SiCDs; (c) schematic diagram of the mechanism for PET process between Fe3+ and SiCDs; (d) FT-IR spectra of SiCDs, SiCDs + Fe3+, SiCDs + Fe3++AA; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of SiCDs upon addition of Fe3+ at different concentrations; (f) the UV–vis adsorption spectra of the SiCDs, SiCDs + Fe3+, AA, SiCDs + Fe3++AA, and SiCDs + Fe3++AA + 1,10-phen and Fe3+ (inset: a photograph of SiCDs + Fe3++AA (I), SiCDs + Fe3++AA + 1,10-phen under visual light (II)). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) FL intensity of SiCDs in the presence of different metal ions; (b) the response of final concentration of Fe3+ to Q E and R E; (c) normalized FL intensity of SiCDs + Fe3+, SiCDs + Fe3++AA at different pH; (d) incubation time versus FL intensity.

(a) FL emission spectra of SiCDs upon addition of Fe3+ at different concentrations; (b) plot of the FL quenching factor (F 0/F 1) vs concentrations of Fe3+; (c) FL emission spectra of SiCDs + Fe3+ upon addition of AA at different concentrations; (d) plot of the FL quenching factor (F 1/F 2) vs concentrations of AA. Inset: the relevant linear region.

Schematic diagram of the construction of SPOC sensor (a); the assembly structure of the optical accessory: i) disassembly diagram of accessories; ii) schematic diagram of light path; iii) the main components (adaptor, element carrier, chip tray, dark box) display diagram (b); the standard curve establishment and sample detection in “AA-Tester” App (c); (d) plot of the FL ratio (I-I 0)/I 0 vs concentrations of AA (i), (ii) linear correlation between the SPOC sensor and FL sensor.
Detected AA contents in various food samples.

row.entry: Concn (mean ± SD, mg/100 g) detected by

head.ce:title: Portable smartphone platform integrated with paper strip-assisted fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive and visual quantitation of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Quality and Technical Supervision, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Quality and Technical Supervision

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Quality and Technical Supervision, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is significant to develop low-cost, sensitive, and portable sensing platform for point-of-care testing of ascorbic acid (AA) in food quality evaluation. Herein, a smart point-of-care (SPOC) sensor, consisting of fluorescent paper chip, 3D printed-accessories and smartphone, was developed for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of AA. Fluorescent paper chip was made through printing silicon doped carbon dots (SiCDs)-Fe3+ as “ink” onto filter paper, in which SiCDs emitting strong fluorescence signal quenched by Fe3+, and then recovered by AA owing to releasing –NH2/–OH and introducing defects on SiCDs. The smartphone with an application named “AA-Tester” was established to analyze AA and exhibited high sensitivity with low detection limits of 18.12 nmol/L. HPLC method was used to verify the SPOC sensor and obtained satisfactory accuracy with relative standard deviations of 0.79–2.31 %. The portable sensing platform integrated fluorescence sensor with smartphone-device will be favorable for ultrasensitive and on-the-spot detection of AA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA, known as vitamin C), a highly water-soluble polyhydroxy compound, plays a dominant role in human health by reducing oxidative damage to proteins, DNA, and lipids for a variety of biological processes (Blokhina, Virolainen, & Fagerstedt, 2003; Gill & Tuteja, 2010). The moderate intake of AA is effective for the common cold, scurvy, mental illness, diarrhea, cancer, AIDS, infertility, and cardiovascular diseases (Tan, An, Pan, Liu, & Hu, 2021). However, the overdose of AA easily results in gastric irritation, diarrhoea and urinary stones (Zhu, Zhao, Nie, Liu, & Yao, 2015). The tolerable limit for daily intake is evaluated at 2000 mg/d of AA (Fong, Chin, & Ng, 2016). As an indispensable micronutrient, AA cannot be synthesized in the organism and must be taken from exogenous sources like fruits or tables to fulfill the body’s requirements (Levine, Rumsey, Daruwala, Park, & Wang, 1999). Considering the vital roles of AA in our daily life, it will be of great significance to monitor and detect its quantities to ensure human health. Conventional approaches involving liquid chromatography, electrophoresis, spectrophotometry, chemiluminescence, titrimetry and fluorometry have been widely investigated and applied to detect AA accurately and sensitively (Nováková, Solich, & Solichová, 2008; Wang et al., 2019). Unfortunately, typical drawbacks (e.g., requiring specialized instrumentation, complicated extraction procedures, cumbersome operation of instruments, and long measurement time) seriously hamper their widespread applications for on-the-spot monitoring of AA. To broaden applications in rural areas and developing countries, it remains a tremendous need to develop novel strategy to realize on-site monitoring of food nutrition.

Compared with conventional methods, smartphone-based portable sensing platforms have notable merits such as low cost, real-time response, simple operation, accurate measurement, and portability, which have been widely applied in various fields, such as clinical diagnostics (Hattori, Sugiura, Wazawa, Matsuda, & Nagai, 2021), environmental monitoring (Xu, Guo, Jia, Zhu, Chen, & Zhao, 2021), and food analysis (Sivakumar & Lee, 2021). Specifically, integrating an attachment allows the built-in smartphone high-quality camera to function as the optical sensor, in which, smartphone readily acquire high-resolution image for accurately extracting image feature information acting as response signal to realize quantify different concentrations of analytes (Rao et al., 2020). Rapid customization of modular function enriches smartphone’s application for user-friendly performing image processing and facilitates real-time data sharing and remote monitoring for stakeholders. Researchers have been actively devoted to compatible fluorometry with a smartphone to develop an on-site sensing platform for quantitative detections. For example, Chu et al. recently develops a portable smartphone-based sensing platform that consists of a fluorescence (FL) sensor integrated with the 3D-printed minidevices and smartphone for selective, sensitive, and real-time/on-site tracing of glutathione (GSH) in human serum (Chu et al., 2020). Jiao et al. demonstrates a 3D-printed, portable, fluorescent-sensing platform for smartphone-capable detection of organophosphorus in vegetables (Jiao et al., 2021). All these cases, the smartphone-based fluorescent device as a conventional spectrofluorimeter has shown comparable analytical performance for point-of-care quantitation. However, the low suitability and sensitivity remain major challenges for more widespread application of this strategy.

Fluorescent paper chips composed of fluorescent probes and paper strips have outstanding features of low-cost, easy to fabricate, store and transport, which provides desired candidate to integrate smartphones for point-of-care (POC) detection, making the sensing process more intuitive and handier (Chu, Wang, Ling, Yu, Yang, & Jiang, 2020). As for sensitivity, the design of fluorescent probes is of great concern. Surface modification with active chemical groups or doping with active atoms on carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes possesses high quantum yields, narrow-band emissions, low toxicity and resistance to photobleaching (Li, Chen, Gooding, & Liu, 2019), making it hold good potential to realize specifical and sensitive targets detection (Su, Ji, & He, 2016). For AA detection, sensitive detection can also be realized with negatively charged ligands (e.g. amino group and mercapto group) modified CDs. In detection process, a switchable fluorescent probe for specifically and sensitively AA detection could be constructed. Harmfulless Fe3+ with positive charges is introduced as a Lewis acid, which acts as electron-acceptor, while negatively charged ligands modified CDs acted as the electron-donor (Cheng, Gondosiswanto, Ciampi, Reece, & Gooding, 2012). The interaction between Fe3+ and negatively charged ligands modified CDs through ligand–metal charge transfer (LMCT) (Xiang, Fang, Leng, & Cao, 2014) can quench the FL of CDs. With the addition of AA, the FL can be recovered ascribed to Fe3+ coordination with two adjacent enol hydroxyl groups at positions C-2 and C-3 of AA (Fong et al., 2016). Therefore, constructing fluorescent paper chip with negatively charged ligands modified CDs holds potential for suitable and sensitive detection of AA.

3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), Glucose (Glc), d-Fructose (Fru), Sucrose (Suc), Aspartic acid (Asp), Cysteine (Cys), Phenylalanine (Phe), Threonine (Thr), Tryptophan (Try), Alanine (Ala), Serine (Ser), Lysine (Lys), Glycine (Gly), Methionine (Met), Glumatic acid (Glu) and GSH were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), Tartaric acid (TA), metaphosphoric acid, phosphoric acid, cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and methanol were obtained from Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CdCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, HgCl2, CoCl2, AgNO3, FeCl2 and Pb(NO3)2 were obtained from Kermel Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Unless otherwise mentioned, all the chemicals were used as obtained without any further purification.

SiCDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.8 g CA and 0.2 g Cys were dissolved in 8 mL deionized water and bubbled with nitrogen gas for 10 min to remove oxygen. Then, 2 mL APTES was injected into the nitrogen-saturated solution. Subsequently, the as-prepared solution was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and incubated at 200 °C for 2 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the resultant mixture was purified through a dialysis bag (100–500 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the prepared SiCDs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The morphology of SiCDs was obtained by using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi-600, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was recorded on a Vetex70 FT-IR Spectroscope (BrukerCorp, Germany) from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected on a D8 ADVANCE A25 X-diffractmeter (Bruker, Germany) from 10° to 70° with Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The absorption spectra and FL spectra of samples were collected on an UV–vis 2550 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 FL Spectrophotometer (Maryland, USA) with the slit widths of excitation/emission being 5/10 nm, respectively. HPLC measurements were carried out by an Agilent 1200LC HPLC (Agilent, USA).

The effects of Fe3+ concentration (0.00–0.45 mmol/L), pH (4.0–10.0) and incubation time (0–300 s) on FL quenching and recovery were tested before detecting AA. Under the optimized conditions, 100 μL diluted SiCDs solution (100-fold), 100 μL acetate buffer solution (0.01 mol/L), 240 μL Fe3+ (1 mmol/L) and a series of AA standard solution of different concentrations (0.00–100.00 μmol/L) or several interferences of metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+ and Fe2+, prepared from their chloride) and biomolecules (Glc, Fru, Suc, GSH, CA, Asp, Cys, Phe, Thr, Try, Ala, Ser, Lys, TA, Gly, Met and Glu) were successively added into a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. The mixed solution was incubated for 2 min at room temperature and the FL spectra were recorded under excitation at 345 nm. All the stock or standard solutions were prepared using ultrapure water.

All food samples were purchased from a local supermarket (Baoding, China). Typically, fresh fruits (pear, apple, hawthorn, kiwi, grape, and orange) were cleaned with tap water and ultrapure water, and dried in air naturally, then weighed accurately and homogenized by using a commercial blender, respectively. After centrifugation in a refrigerated centrifuge at 3000 rpm for 20 min to prevent oxidation, the obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm PES membrane and diluted to appropriate concentration in the detection range with ultrapure water. Specifically, the final juices of pear, apple, hawthorn, kiwi, grape, and orange were diluted to 1, 1, 10, 20, 1, and 15 times, respectively. Afterwards, the FL assay was conducted in the same as described Section 2.4. In addition, the applicability of this method was further verified by the HPLC method. All experiments were performed in triplicate and the basic statistics were calculated using the Minitab 21.0 software (Minitab Inc., USA). A student’s t-test was calculated for difference significance tests (P > 0.05) between different groups.

The FL paper strips were designed with a microarray pattern using Microsoft WORD and printed onto A4 size Whatman filter papers (210 mm × 297 mm) with a L3153 printer (Epson, Japan) using normal cartridge ink, which forms a hydrophobic barrier upon heating at 180 °C for 3 min. Each indicator area is a circle with a diameter of 5 mm. The SiCDs-Fe3+ solution (100-fold diluted SiCDs solution with Fe3+ of 0.16 mmol/L) worked as a kind of “ink” was printed on filter paper to prepare fluorescent paper strips, which was injected into a vacant cartridge to be printed in the whole indicator areas. To ensure that the fluorescent probe was evenly distributed on the paper strips, the printing process was repeated 20 times. Then the fluorescent filter paper was cut into paper strips with a size of 42 mm × 15.5 mm to contain 1 × 7 indicator areas. In the actual detection process, the fluorescent paper strips were dripped with different concentrations of AA solution and the sample solution. Finally, the prepared fluorescent paper strips were placed in a dry plastic case for future use.

A SPOC sensor had 3D printed attachments to connect the fluorescent paper strips and smartphone, attachments were constructed by the CR-20 3D printer (Creality, China), including an adaptor (73.1 mm × 73.1 mm, with a hole of 32.0 mm diameter) connected with a smartphone (Huawei Mate 30 Pro), a dark box (80.2 mm × 73.1 mm × 73.1 mm), two element carrier (22.5 mm × 15.2 mm × 15.2 mm) as micro-ultraviolet LED holders, a chip tray (15.5 mm × 42.0 mm) for fluorescent paper strips, a chip tray cover and a baseplate, which effectively avoided edge effect induced by surrounding environment and internal light source.

The morphology of the as-synthesized SiCDs was studied by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the SiCDs were well mono-dispersed. The lattice fringes were obviously viewed and the d-spacing value was about 0.25 nm. Furthermore, XRD pattern (Fig. 1b) of SiCDs represented one single diffraction peak at about 2θ = 22°, indicating that the SiCDs were amorphous (Han et al., 2018). XPS measurement was performed to further expound the surface groups and chemical composition of SiCDs. Fig. 1c demonstrated the existence of O, N, C, S and Si elements at the peaks of 531.9, 398.8, 284.8, 168.8 and 102.2 eV in the survey spectrum. The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum of SiCDs delivered three strong peaks at binding energies of 532.37, 531.77, and 530.97 eV (Fig. 1d), corresponding to C–O, Si–O and Si–O groups, respectively (Han et al., 2018). The N 1s region (Fig. 1e) could be fitted with three main components associated with Si–N (401.17 eV), C–N (400.07 eV) and N–H (399.47 eV) (Han, Chen, Feng, Liu, Ma, & Chen, 2017). The characteristic peaks of C–O, C–N, C–C and C–Si were observed at 287.97, 285.77, 284.80 and 284.47 eV (Ding, Wang, Chai, Qiang, Shi, & Yang, 2019), respectively, in C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f). Four dominant peaks at 102.67, 102.27, 101.87 and 99.47 eV in Si 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g) were attributed to Si–O, Si–N, Si–C and Si (0) (Q. Li et al., 2016), which confirmed the existence of silicon core. For S 2p spectrum, peaks at 163.37 and 164.07 eV were assigned to the 2-oxidation state of S. And peaks at 167.73 eV (2p 3/2) and 168.85 eV (2p 1/2) (Fig. 1h) were probably ascribed to the S2O3 2− group, which was possibly generated by oxidation resulted from high reaction temperature in the experiment process (X. Wang, Wu, Jiang, Wang, & Zhang, 2017). The obtained results suggested the richness of functional hydrophilic groups such as –NH2/–OH/–SH on the surface of as-synthesized SiCDs, which might promote the hydrophilicity and stability of SiCDs, and facilitate their detection performance in the aqueous system.

The optical properties of the as-fabricated SiCDs were investigated with UV–vis and FL spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 1i showed that SiCDs exhibited a large absorption peak located at 240 nm and a characteristic absorption peak at 345 nm. The former was ascribed to π–π* transition of C–N or C–O, and the later was assigned to the n–π* transition of CO or C–OH groups of SiCDs (Nsanzamahoro et al., 2019). The FL spectra depicted in Fig. 1i showed a symmetric emission (Em) located at 425 nm with the excitation (Ex) of 345 nm, displaying a large Stokes shift of 80 nm. The resultant SiCDs exhibited a transparent color in daylight and intensively blue FL under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 1i, inset). The quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized SiCDs was calculated to be 30.84 % (Fig. S1), the significant FL intensity might reasonably correspond to quantum-size effect and high probability of producing a defect-free structure of such size (J. Wang, Li, Long, & Li, 2016), which was conducive to serve as a highly sensitive FL sensor for potential applications.

In quest of further confirming the excitation-independent FL behavior of SiCDs, several FL emission spectra at excitation wavelengths from 320 to 360 nm were traced and displayed in Fig. S2. The FL Em intensity gradually increased to the maximum at 345 nm along with the corresponding emission of 425 nm. The excitation-independent FL behavior was attributed to the π*→n transitions of the surface-attached functionalities (Si–O/C–N/C–S/C–O), affecting the band gap of SiCDs (Gong, Li, Hu, Zhou, Shuang, & Dong, 2017), and thus lowering the interference of auto-FL during analysis. As shown in Fig. S3, SiCDs simultaneously exhibited prominent stabilities in a wide pH (5.0–10.0), excellent salt-tolerance (NaCl solution, 0.0–1.0 mol/L), high thermostability (4–65 °C), excellent resistance to photobleaching (consecutive irradiation for 3500 s) and favorable storage stability (6 months). The stabilities of SiCDs were detailedly discussed in Supplementary Material . The prominent FL performance of SiCDs showed their potential application possibilities in food analysis.

Another possible mechanism for the FL quenching was attributed to PET, which could be due to that Fe3+ combined with the–NH2/–SH/–OH on the surface of SiCDs, leading to a nonradiative electron transformation from the excited state of the SiCDs to Fe3+ (Gong et al., 2017). Specifically, Fe3+ could preferentially bind with o-quinone-containing ligands or amino groups of SiCDs to form strong coordination complex. The electronegative heteroatoms such as O (electronegativity of 3.44), N (electronegativity of 3.05) and S (electronegativity of 2.58) atoms in SiCDs could effectively tune the electronic local densities and donated their lone-pair electrons to Fe3+ (S. Li, Li, Cao, Zhu, & Fan, 2014), which promoted the coordination interaction simultaneously, leading to the significant FL quenching. In order to further certify our conjecture, we investigated the electronic band gap (Eg ) and valence band (VB) of SiCDs (Fig. 2c). The direct electronic Eg of SiCDs was calculated to be 2.73 eV (Fig. S4a), VB was 0.88 eV versus NHE (Fig. S4b), and conduction band (CB) (-1.85 eV) versus NHE was obtained according to the formula: E CB = E VB − E g. The electrode potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ (0.77 eV) (Rong, Song, Zhao, Yao, Wang, & Chen, 2015) was located between CB and VB of SiCDs, therefore the electron of VB was excited to the d orbit of Fe3+ instead of CB, resulting in FL quenching via PET (Guo, Yue, Huang, Liu, Zeng, & Wang, 2018). FT-IR measurements were performed to further investigate the FL quenching mechanism. The surface functional groups of SiCDs and SiCDs-Fe3+ were analyzed by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2d). The characteristic broad absorption bands of SiCDs at 3450 and 3384 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 (Li et al., 2019), which shifted to 3442 and 3375 cm−1 after introduction of Fe3+. The peaks of SiCDs at 1696 cm−1 (CO), 1633 cm−1 (N–H) and 1405 cm−1 (C–N/N–H/C–O) (Cheng et al., 2012; Guo et al., 2018; Wu & Tong, 2019) shifted to 1688 cm−1, 1621 cm−1, 1398 cm−1 after introduction of Fe3+. The peak at 1271 cm−1 (C–N) disappeared. Two peaks at 692 cm−1 (C–S) and 2561 cm−1 (–SH) (Behray et al., 2016; Chou et al., 2013) shifted to 697 cm−1 and 2565 cm−1. These changes indicated that Fe3+ coordinated with –NH2/–OH/–SH of SiCDs. On the other hand, the UV–vis absorption spectra of SiCDs were recorded upon gradually increasing concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 2e), the absorption intensity increased without obvious shift in wavelength, which was in accordance with the tendency of the FL quenching efficiency observed for Fe3+. Above all, the synergistic effects of IFE and PET accounted for FL quenching of Fe3+ based on SiCDs.

As mentioned above (refer to Fig. 2a), AA could recover the quenched-FL of SiCDs-Fe3+. AA had strong reduction ability due to the existing enediol group, which easily oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) with the addition of Fe3+. Fe2+ originated from Fe3+ reduction, proved by the UV–vis absorption (Fig. 2f), could inappreciably recover the FL of SiCDs for its poor chelating ability to SiCDs. In addition, FT-IR spectra of SiCDs-Fe3+ before and after AA addition were performed to further exploring the recovery mechanism. The characteristic peaks of SiCDs + Fe3+ at 3442 and 3375 cm−1 (refer to Fig. 2c), assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 (Li et al., 2019), shifted to 3535 and 3384 cm−1 after introduction of AA. The C–N stretching vibrations at 1260 cm−1 reappeared. The peaks at 1688 cm−1 (CO) and 1621 cm−1 (N–H) shifted to 1639 cm−1 and 1617 cm−1. Those results demonstrated that –NH2/–OH were released. The exposure of –NH2/–OH could be able to introduce different defects on the surface of SiCDs, acting as excitation energy traps and contributing to FL recovery (Lai et al., 2013). In conclusion, the FL of SiCDs could be quenched by Fe3+ through IFE and PET, and restored by AA on account of redox reaction and energy traps induced by exposure of –NH2/–OH.

The selective of FL quenchers was crucial to construct “switch-on” fluorescent sensor for sensing AA. Fig. 3 a showed the relative change in the FL intensity of SiCDs in the presence of various metal ions. Fe3+ significantly quenched the FL of SiCDs, while other metal ions had negligible effect, which could be attributed to that Fe3+ selectively coordinated with the–NH2/–SH/–OH on the surface of SiCDs (Guo et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Raveendran, Babu, & Renuka, 2019; Wang, Luo, Jing, Yang, Huo, & Wang, 2021), indicating that Fe3+ could be a desirable candidate to construct “switch-on” fluorescent sensor. The concentration of Fe3+ was vitally important to promote the analytical performance in AA detection on account of the lack or excess of Fe3+ leading to low quenching efficiency (Q E) and recovery efficiency (R E), which was not conducive to detecting AA sensitively (Gao et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2018). Q E and R E were used to evaluate the dependence of Fe3+ concentration for AA detection, which was calculated according to equations (1) and (2).
(1)
Q E
( % )
=
F 0
-
F 1
/
F 0
× 100
(2)
R E
( % )
=
F 2
-
F 1
/
(
F 0
-
F 1
)
× 100
where F 1 and F 0 were the FL intensities of SiCDs system in the presence and absence of Fe3+. F 2 was recovered FL intensity of SiCDs at 435 nm after introducing AA.

As shown in Fig. 3b, Q E increased sharply with the increased concentration of Fe3+ from 0.00 mmol/L to 0.16 mmol/L and remained almost invariable beyond 0.16 mmol/L, demonstrating that the amount of Fe3+ bonding with SiCDs gradually increased to saturation. When AA was added, R E reached the maximum of ∼34 % with the Fe3+ concentration at 0.16 mmol/L. Hence, 0.16 mmol/L Fe3+ was chosen for sensing AA.

Then, the effect of pH on detection of AA was investigated. Fig. 3c showed that the normalized FL intensity of SiCDs-Fe3+ decreased when the pH increased from 4.0 to 5.0 and an unapparent FL change was observed at the pH of 5.0–7.0, while the FL intensity increased with the pH ranged 7.0–10.0. The increasing FL intensity in alkaline medium could be due to that Fe3+ could form insoluble iron hydroxide owing to its poor stability and prevented the coordination of Fe3+ to the amino and carboxyl groups of SiCDs. Thus, Fe3+ could not effectively quench the FL intensity of SiCDs at alkaline medium. In addition, AA was a kind of unsaturated polyhydroxylactone compound, which was easily oxidized, especially in alkaline media. Taking the quenching/recovery effect into account, pH of 5.0 was selected for subsequent experiments.

To obtain the high FL response performance of the fabricated SiCDs, the effects of incubating time on Fe3+ and AA were studied. As shown in Fig. 3d, the FL intensity of SiCDs decreased sharply after adding 0.16 mmol/L Fe3+ and tended to stabilize after 30 s, which was ascribed to the abundant functional groups (–NH2/–OH/–SH) on the surface of SiCDs interacted rapidly with Fe3+ and led to the tremendous FL quenching. When AA was added to the SiCDs-Fe3+ system, the FL intensity of SiCDs increased within 120 s. And the FL intensity almost kept constant even if the reaction time exceeded 120 s, implying 120 s was enough for AA to reduce Fe3+ and recover the FL of SiCDs. Therefore, the FL quenching time of 30 s and recovery time of 120 s were applied for further experiments. Based on the above discussion, the concentration of 0.16 mmol/L Fe3+, the pH of 5.0, the quenching time of 30 s and recovery time of 120 s were utilized for subsequent FL measurements.

To ensure the presented FL sensing strategy could be used for sensitive quantification, the analytical performance of the SiCDs was investigated under optimization. As mentioned before, Fe3+ quenched FL of SiCDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , the FL intensities of SiCDs decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 0.001–166.70 μmol/L (Fig. 4a). The FL response (F 0 /F 1) of SiCDs was linearly dependent on the concentrations of Fe3+ from 0.001 to 166.70 μmol/L and the linear regression equation was expressed as F 0 /F 1 = 0.042 C Fe + 0.889 with a correlation coefficient (R2 ) of 0.9996 (Fig. 4b), close to the Stern–Volmer equation (3).
(3)
F 0
F 1
=
K
SV
C
Fe
+ 1
where F 0 and F denoted the FL intensities of SiCDs before and after Fe3+ incorporation, respectively. C Fe was the concentration of Fe3+, and KSV was the Stern–Volmer constant. The K SV value was calculated to be 0.042 μM−1, proving the high quenching efficiency of Fe3+ and the potential ability for detecting AA.

Furthermore, the FL intensities of SiCDs-Fe3+ increased with the AA concentrations changing from 0.001 to 22.33 μmol/L (Fig. 4c). The FL response (F 1 /F 2) of SiCDs-Fe3+ was evaluated by the Stern–Volmer Eq. (4).
(4)
F 1
F 2
=
K
SV
′
C
AA
+ 1
where F 1 denoted the FL intensities of SiCDs-Fe3+ (0.16 mmol/L of Fe3+). F 2 was the FL intensities of SiCDs-Fe3+ after AA addition. C AA was the concentration of AA, and K’ SV was the Stern–Volmer constant of SiCDs-Fe3+. Fig. 4d showed the FL intensity versus the concentration of AA, which was fitted into two linear equations: F 1 /F 2 = −0.1447 C AA + 0.9693 (R2 = 0.9995) and F 1 /F 2 = −0.0210 C AA + 0.5903 (R2 = 0.9953) with AA concentration of 0.001–3.00 and 3.00–10.67 μmol/L, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.16 nmol/L calculated as following equation (5).
(5)
LOD =
3 σ
/ k
where k was the slop of the calibration curve, and σ was consistent with the standard deviation of a blank (n = 15). Furthermore, for AA determination, the analytical capability of the SiCDs-Fe3+ system was compared with those of other previously reported systems, which was exhibited in Table S2. The SiCDs-Fe3+ FL probe possessed a much lower LOD compared with others. Moreover, the response speed of this switch-on sensor was much faster than numerous previous reports, implying that this method was desirable for sensitive and efficient analysis of AA.

Target selectivity was another key characteristic to estimate whether the SiCDs fluorescent sensor could be used in real samples. The selective detection of AA by the SiCDs-Fe3+ system from a group of metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+ and Fe2+) and biomolecules (Glc, Fru, Suc, GSH, CA, Asp, Cys, Phe, Thr, Try, Ala, Ser, Lys, TA, Gly, Met and Glu) was depicted in Fig. S5. It could be observed that only AA obviously recovered the FL intensity of SiCDs-Fe3+ up to a remarkable extent. GSH, CA and TA displayed weak FL recovery, which was ascribed to carboxylic structures and α-hydroxyl on GSH, CA and TA promoting reduction (Huang et al., 2016; Luo et al., 2018). And other substances had negligible FL recovery, which clearly indicated that the constructed fluorescent sensor had excellent selectivity towards AA. The excellent specific recognition ability combined with high sensitivity and fast response of SiCDs-Fe3+ to AA envisioned that the proposed method might be directly applied to sensing AA in real food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AA in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring AA in food samples was indispensably crucial for human health. A series of assay were conducted to evaluate the practical feasibility of the as-proposed SiCDs-based FL sensor for detection of AA in common food samples (pear, apple, hawthorn, kiwi, grape, orange, orange juice and peach juice). As shown in Table 1 , the low relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.79–2.31 % were achieved, indicating high accuracy. Additionally, the routine HPLC method was used to validate the results obtained by the fluorescent sensing platform. Fig. S6 showed the chromatograms of AA with retention times of 6.73 min. The results revealed the satisfactory consistencies between HPLC and SiCDs-Fe3+ FL sensor (P > 0.05), indicating that the proposed SiCDs FL sensor possessed acceptable reliability and accuracy and could be used for practical detection of AA in food matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and application of smart point-of-care sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The previous FL paper-based sensors were only limited to qualitative analysis of AA, and the quantitatively determine had challenge. The SPOC sensor potentially applied for quantitative detection of AA (Fig. 5 a). The SPOC sensor consisted by 3D printed attachments, the fluorescent paper strips and smartphone (Fig. 5b, corresponding profile displayed in Fig. S7). The APP, AA-Tester (Fig. 5c), was designed for Android platform and installed on a smartphone (Huawei Mate 30 Pro). The APP extracted the RGB information from digital images captured by a smartphone and converted into a gray value (I) for calculation by floating point method (6):
(6)
I = R × 0.299 + G × 0.587 + B × 0.114
where R was red, G was green, and B was blue. The APP was self-developed and based on the Java programming language. The IntelliJ IDEA platform and the HBuilderX platform were used to pack and release. The APP includes four functions: new test, standard curve, historical data and uploads to the cloud. Through this visual and quantitative test platform, the relationship between color information and AA concentration was determined (Fig. 5c and Video S1).

The SPOC sensor automatically analyzed the RGB value and calculated the gray value I and the ratio (I-I 0 )/I 0 to establish the standard curve, where I0 and I were the gray value of the test strips before and after adding AA. The gray ratio (I-I 0 )/I 0 of AA was linearly dependent on the concentrations of AA and the linear regression equation was expressed as (I-I 0 )/I 0 = 0.4807C AA-0.0176 with R2 = 0.9995, and the LOD was about 18.12 nmol/L (Fig. 5d(i)). To be more specific, we also generated a correlation plot between the SPOC sensor and FL sensor (Fig. 5d(ii)) to demonstrate that those two sensors correlated well. The linear regression equation was expressed as Y = 0.9973X + 0.0199 with a R2 of 0.9977, where X and Y represented the result of the SPOC sensor and FL sensor respectively. Thence, the accurate determination of AA concentration in samples could be achieved by the cooperation of software and hardware.

In summary, a mobile minidevice, including FL paper strip, 3D printed-accessories and smartphone in conjunction with a custom “AA-Tester” APP had been developed as SPOC sensor for visual quantitative analysis of AA at the trace level. Fluorescent paper chip was prepared through printing SiCDs-Fe3+ as “ink” onto filter paper, in which SiCDs and Fe3+ act as FL signal and quencher, respectively. The FL of SiCDs could be specifically rapidly quenched by Fe3+due to the electron of –NH2/−SH/−OH on the surface of SiCDs transferred to Fe3+. Once adding AA, the FL of SiCDs-Fe3+ rapidly reversed due to introduction of different defects on the surface of SiCDs to reduce Fe3+. The FL color variation was captured by smartphone and digitized with self-designed “AA-Tester” APP. The SPOC sensor exhibited excellent sensitivity toward quantitation of AA with low LOD of 18.12 nmol/L. The proposed tactic possessed excellent performance of rapid response for detecting AA with satisfactory accuracy. Due to the excellent performance in portability, economy, response time and sensitivity, the novel SPOC sensor showed remarkable potential for application in food safety.

Chunhua Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Xiao Xu: Investigation, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Fuli Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yue Zhao: Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yubo Shi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation. Xin Zhao: Formal analysis, Validation. Jue Liu: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University (521000981407).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A smartphone-integrated method for visual detection of tetracycline
Chemical Engineering Journal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, J., Guo, S., Jia, L., Zhu, T., Chen, X., & Zhao, T. (2021). A smartphone-integrated method for visual detection of tetracycline. Chemical Engineering Journal, 416, 127741. doi: 10.1016/j.cej.2020.127741.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-redox modulated fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective ascorbic acid detection with highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles via solid-state synthesis
Analytical Chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, X., Zhao, T., Nie, Z., Liu, Y., & Yao, S. (2015). Non-redox modulated fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective ascorbic acid detection with highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles via solid-state synthesis. Analytical Chemistry, 87(16), 8524-8530. doi: 10.1021/acs.analchem.5b02167.
